result: I keep thinking about the way light pools on my kitchen floor at 4:17 p.m., a gold so specific it feels like the day is handing me a receipt for the hours I’ve already spent. I never asked for proof, yet here it is, luminous and indisputable. I stand barefoot in that warm rectangle and feel time become liquid, soaking into the pine boards, into the soles of my feet, into the blood that keeps revising me cell by cell. Outside, the neighbor kid practices the same violin squeak she’s been murdering since September; inside, I become the echo of every afternoon I’ve ever wasted believing there would be a better version of the moment arriving soon. The cat flicks her tail twice, a metronome against the couch, and suddenly I remember being seven, licking cake batter from a plastic spoon, convinced the bowl was bottomless. I want to crawl inside that memory the way one crawls under a quilt made by hands that no longer exist, but the light is shifting, withdrawing its signature from the floorboards millimetre by millimetre. I think: if I stand still enough, I can keep it. I think: if I breathe less, time will forget to collect its change. The rectangle shrinks to a postage stamp, then a coin, then the bright eye of a creature that refuses to blink first. I lose. Evening claims the room, the cat, the music next door. I pocket the receipt anyway—evidence that once, for one measurable minute, I was rich enough to trade an entire day for a single slab of light.

Outside, the neighbor kid practices the same violin squeak she’s been murdering since September; inside, I become the echo of every afternoon I’ve ever wasted believing there would be a better version of the moment arriving soon. The cat flicks her tail twice, a metronome against the couch, and suddenly I remember being seven, licking cake batter from a plastic spoon, convinced the bowl was bottomless. I want to crawl inside that memory the way one crawls under a quilt made by hands that no longer exist, but the light is shifting, withdrawing its signature from the floorboards millimetre by millimetre. I think: if I stand still enough, I can keep it. I think: if I breathe less, time will forget to collect its change. The rectangle shrinks to a postage stamp, then a coin, then the bright eye of a creature that refuses to blink first. I lose. Evening claims the room, the cat, the music next door. I pocket the receipt anyway—evidence that once, for one measurable minute, I was rich enough to trade an entire day for a single slab of light.
